channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Steam Being Sued by UK Performing Right Society Over Music Rights
item.content:encoded:
The UK's Performing Right Society (PRS) has initiated a lawsuit against Steam, alleging that Valve's platform is playing host to games that use music for which Steam does not have the rights.

As reported by Gamesindustry.biz, PRS claims that Valve "never obtained a license for its use of the rights managed by PRS on behalf of its members", suggesting that the platform has many games on it which use PRS members' music without their permission.

PRS for Music CCO Dan Gopal says that PRS "exists to protect the value of [its members'] work with integrity, transparency, and fairness", and that the songwriters and creatives behind video game soundtracks "deserve to have their contributions recognized and fairly valued".

Specifically, PRS' claim pertains to "game titles which incorporate PRS members' musical works", like Forza Horizon and Grand Theft Auto, as well as other games with licensed soundtracks rather than original compositions (although the aforementioned games do feature those as well).

According to PRS, it's been looking to license its works with Valve "for many years", but Valve hasn't shown "appropriate engagement". PRS says its lawsuit will proceed unless Valve "engages positively with discussions" and obtains the necessary license to cover PRS members' songs.

The licensing issue here is a bit of a strange one. While it's up to publishers to cover the licensing for their games, that doesn't apply to platforms on which they appear, at least not according to UK copyright law.

As noted by Music Ally's Stuart Dredge, making music available on certain platforms is a separate issue to including it in games, and that's the distinction which is at the core of this lawsuit.

Given that Steam broke its concurrent player record in January, with over 42 million players logging in simultaneously at that time, it's safe to say Valve's platform is still the big dog when it comes to PC gaming.

However, between this lawsuit, objections from indie devs regarding racist reviews, and games being banned with seemingly no explanation, the platform is very much not without its controversies as well.

We'll have to wait and see how this PRS lawsuit plays out, but the ramifications of any eventual decision could be far-reaching for the industry. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/uk-performing-right-society-steam-lawsuit
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Yoshi and the Mysterious Book Release Date Revealed for May
item.content:encoded:
Nintendo has revealed the Yoshi and the Mysterious Book release date, so get ready to embark on yet another irresistibly adorable adventure alongside everyone's favorite green dinosaur pal.

As revealed in a new trailer, Yoshi and the Mysterious Book will be launching exclusively on Switch 2 on May 21st (sorry, original Switch owners). As a reminder, the game was originally announced and shown off as part of Nintendo's September Direct presentation.

The new trailer also gives us some more information about exactly what kind of game Yoshi and the Mysterious Book will be, and while it certainly looks like other Yoshi platformers, this one has a more discovery-focused bent to it.

As you make your way through Yoshi and the Mysterious Book's levels, you'll encounter all manner of creatures and other phenomena, and when you make a discovery about what those things do, these discoveries will be added to encyclopedic character Mr. E's database.

One creature, for instance, spits bubbles from its mouth, and consuming the creature will allow Yoshi to do so himself. Don't think too hard about the mechanics (or, indeed, the ethics) of that statement.

If that creature happens to consume nearby apples, its bubbles will grow even bigger, allowing you to reach hitherto-inaccessible areas. It looks like this one will be all about experimentation and observation, which is a neat twist for a platformer to have.

You can check out the new trailer for Yoshi and the Mysterious Book right here. It shows off the game's aesthetic, as well as its rather charming-looking core gameplay loop.

Yoshi and the Mysterious Book launches exclusively for Nintendo Switch 2 on May 21st, and it's not the only game slated to arrive on the console this spring, so you should have plenty to look forward to as a Switch 2 owner. Stay tuned for more on this and all other things Nintendo-related.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph

item.title: Resident Evil Requiem Story DLC In the Works Alongside New "Minigame"
item.content:encoded:
Resident Evil Requiem is getting itself a story expansion in the future, director Koshi Nakanishi has confirmed, and there's more on the way for the horror game as well.

In a new message on social media, Nakanishi first thanks fans for Resident Evil Requiem's recent five million sales milestone, which the game managed to achieve less than a week after its release. Considering it took the Resident Evil 4 remake three weeks to get to four million, that's impressive.

After thanking fans for Requiem's sales, Nakanishi then pledges to continue supporting the game in terms of bug fixes and performance improvements, and he also says that the game's "much-awaited" photo mode is on the way, although he doesn't say exactly when it's coming.

Nakanishi goes on to say that there's "another surprise" coming around May in the form of a "minigame", as well as announcing the aforementioned Resident Evil Requiem story DLC. Again, no further information was provided for either.

We might be able to get a couple of clues about the minigame from the pieces of paper Nakanishi holds up during the DLC's announcement, however. Briefly, we see a shot depicting something with the title "Forbidden Requiem", showing protagonist Leon in a suit alongside an unknown female character.

Humorously, we also see what appears to be Requiem's title art with cat faces replacing those of the protagonists, as well as a seemingly unrelated shot of a chef and a barbecue. Finally, though, there's a shot of antagonist Victor Gideon at a Buckshot Roulette or Inscryption-style table. Could this be a tease?

In any case, Nakanishi says the upcoming Resident Evil Requiem story DLC will "take some time", so it's probably going to be a little while before we hear any more about it.

In the meantime, you can pick up Resident Evil Requiem right now on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and Switch 2. You can check out our very own Andrew Stretch's review right here.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
